footballers.name: Christian Vieri
footballers.story: A nomadic Italian striker raised in Sydney who became the world's most expensive player when Inter signed him for в‚¬49m in 1999. Bullish, two-footed, devastating in the air вЂ” defined the Inter attack of the early 2000s alongside Ronaldo. Played for seven Serie A clubs, never quite settling, but scored everywhere he went.
footballers.numbers: 194 club goals В· 23 in 49 for Italy В· 4 goals at the 2002 World Cup
didYouKnow: Grew up in Sydney and wanted to be a cricketer. Idolised Allan Border.
Won a Ferrari off AtlГ©tico president JesГєs Gil for a hat-trick against PAOK вЂ” never collected it because he felt guilty about leaving the club a year later.
Successfully sued Inter and Telecom Italia in 2012 after discovering owner Massimo Moratti had him under phone surveillance.

footballers.name: Marc Overmars
footballers.story: Lightning-quick Dutch winger who tore up three leagues in three countries. Ajax's Champions League hero, Arsenal's double-winning game-changer, and one of Barcelona's first marquee Premier League imports. Blew his ACL clean off the bone in 1995 and somehow came back faster.
footballers.numbers: 78 club goals В· 17 in 86 for the Netherlands
didYouKnow: Suffered a completely severed cruciate ligament in 1995 and recovered to anchor Arsenal's 1997вЂ“98 Double-winning side.
Announced his ВЈ25m move to Barcelona on his personal website before telling the press вЂ” got 250,000 hits in a day, an internet-era footballing first.
Resigned as Ajax's director of football in 2022 after admitting to sending inappropriate messages to female colleagues; signed with Antwerp weeks later amid sponsor backlash.

footballers.name: Jason Koumas
footballers.countryFlag: рџЏґу Ѓ§у Ѓўу Ѓ·у Ѓ¬у Ѓіу Ѓї
footballers.story: The Welsh playmaker whose feet were fluent in a language his career never quite spoke. Mercurial, brilliant on his day, infuriatingly anonymous on others. The Championship's outstanding talent of the mid-2000s вЂ” he should have done more, and everyone knew it.
honours: Championship Player of the Year вЂ” 2006вЂ“07
PFA Team of the Year Г— 4 (between 2001вЂ“02 and 2006вЂ“07)
Cardiff City Player of the Year вЂ” 2005вЂ“06
footballers.numbers: 68 goals in 422 club appearances В· 10 in 34 for Wales
didYouKnow: Came through the Liverpool academy alongside Steven Gerrard and Michael Owen вЂ” left because they wouldn't play him centrally.
The Guardian once argued his raw talent was "no less than Gerrard's" and blamed his career on being "undisciplined".
His son Lewis came through the Liverpool academy and made his senior debut for the club in February 2024.

footballers.name: Jay-Jay Okocha
footballers.story: So good they named him twice. The Nigerian magician who turned the Reebok Stadium into a circus tent вЂ” flicks, rabonas, no-look passes вЂ” and somehow kept Bolton in the Premier League while doing it. The patron saint of unnecessary skill.
honours: Africa Cup of Nations вЂ” Nigeria, 1994
footballers.numbers: 89 club goals В· 14 in 73 for Nigeria
didYouKnow: Took Turkish citizenship at FenerbahГ§e and adopted the playing name "Muhammed Yavuz" in tribute to a club staff member.
Mentored a young Ronaldinho at PSG, who later credited him as a stylistic influence.
The "so good they named him twice" line wasn't a slogan вЂ” Bolton printed it on the back of replica shirts.

footballers.name: Yakubu Aiyegbeni
footballers.story: The Yak. Built like a wardrobe, finished like a fox. Quietly one of the Premier League's most efficient strikers of his era вЂ” Portsmouth, Boro, Everton вЂ” racking up goals while half the league forgot how good he actually was.
honours: Israeli Premier League Г— 2 вЂ” Maccabi Haifa
Football League First Division вЂ” Portsmouth, 2002вЂ“03
footballers.numbers: 170+ league goals across his career В· 21 in 58 for Nigeria
didYouKnow: Scored seven in eight Champions League games for Maccabi Haifa in 2002вЂ“03 вЂ” including a hat-trick against Olympiakos вЂ” which earned him his Portsmouth move.
Scored all four in Blackburn's 4вЂ“2 win over Swansea in December 2011 вЂ” his first four-goal haul since the Pompey days.
Played barefoot on the streets of Benin City until his brother bought him his first pair of boots aged 12.

footballers.name: IvГЎn Campo
footballers.story: The wild-haired Spaniard who walked out of Real Madrid's Champions League winners' photo and into a Sam Allardyce midfield at Bolton вЂ” and looked, somehow, completely at home. A two-time European champion playing route one football in the rain at the Reebok.
honours: Champions League Г— 2 вЂ” Real Madrid, 1999вЂ“2000 & 2001вЂ“02
footballers.numbers: 21 goals in 384 club appearances
didYouKnow: Sam Allardyce reckoned Campo's long-range strike against Spurs in August 2006 had won Goal of the Season before the season had really started.
Battled an anxiety disorder during his Madrid years, undergoing therapy that he later credited with rescuing his career.
Loaned to Bolton in 2003 expecting to return to Madrid вЂ” instead signed permanently because he simply preferred living in Bolton.

footballers.name: Rui Costa
footballers.story: The understated Portuguese conductor of Fiorentina's late-90s side and Milan's Champions League winners. All grace, weight of pass, and quiet authority вЂ” the kind of number 10 who made everyone around him better and never quite got the credit for it.
footballers.numbers: 66 club goals В· 26 in 94 for Portugal В· Euro 2004 finalist
didYouKnow: The only red card of his entire career came in 1997 вЂ” for walking off the pitch too slowly during a World Cup qualifier, which cost Portugal qualification.
Starred in Nike's iconic 1996 "Good vs Evil" commercial alongside Ronaldo and Cantona вЂ” the Roman amphitheatre one.
Has been President of Benfica since October 2021, elected with 84% of the vote.

footballers.name: Andrew Johnson
footballers.countryFlag: рџЏґу Ѓ§у Ѓўу ЃҐу Ѓ®у Ѓ§у Ѓї
footballers.story: The relentless Palace striker who outran Premier League defences for two seasons and very nearly carried Selhurst Park on his back. Quick, brave, fond of going down вЂ” depending on who you asked. Twenty-one league goals in his debut Premier League season at a relegated club.
honours: First Division Play-off вЂ” Birmingham, 2002 / Palace, 2004
PFA Team of the Year вЂ” 2003вЂ“04, 2004вЂ“05
Premier League Player of the Month вЂ” Oct 2004 & Sep 2006
footballers.numbers: 114 club goals В· 8 in 8 for England
didYouKnow: Was eligible for Poland through his grandfather вЂ” Sven-GГ¶ran Eriksson handed him an England debut almost immediately to prevent it.
Mourinho publicly branded him "untrustworthy" in 2006; the PGMOL later defended him as unfairly tarred with the diving brush.
Voted into Crystal Palace's Centenary XI in 2005 вЂ” the only active player in the team.

footballers.name: David Trezeguet
footballers.story: Argentine-born, French-made. Ten years at Juventus with one job вЂ” finish вЂ” done at a level few have matched. The man whose volley won France Euro 2000 and whose loyalty stuck through Calciopoli when most of the dressing room walked.
footballers.numbers: 227 club goals В· 34 in 71 for France В· Juve's all-time leading foreign scorer
didYouKnow: Scored the golden goal in extra time of the Euro 2000 final against Italy вЂ” France's last-minute winner.
Stayed at Juventus when they were relegated to Serie B after Calciopoli, while most of the squad left for European football.
Surpassed Omar SГ­vori's 167 goals on 9 December 2009 to become Juve's highest-scoring foreign player ever.

footballers.name: Jan Koller
footballers.story: Two metres and two centimetres of Czech striker. "Dino." Borussia Dortmund's aerial nightmare, the Czech Republic's all-time top scorer, and вЂ” somehow вЂ” a former goalkeeper who once kept a clean sheet between the sticks for Dortmund mid-match.
honours: Belgian First Division Г— 2 вЂ” Anderlecht
footballers.numbers: 199 club goals В· 55 in 91 for the Czech Republic (still the record)
didYouKnow: Went in goal for Dortmund in 2002 after their keeper was sent off and all subs were used вЂ” kept a clean sheet for the remaining 23 minutes and was named the Bundesliga's goalkeeper of the week.
Started his career as a goalkeeper before being converted to striker вЂ” explains the in-game cameo.
Stood 2.02m / 6'8" вЂ” universally nicknamed "Dino" because he was taller than the dressing room.

footballers.name: Adel Taarabt
footballers.story: QPR's mercurial Moroccan вЂ” the most outrageously talented Championship player of the early 2010s, dragging Loftus Road back to the Premier League almost single-handed. Disappeared, reinvented himself as a deep-lying midfielder at Benfica, won a Portuguese title in his thirties.
honours: Championship Player of the Year вЂ” 2010вЂ“11
PFA Championship Team of the Year вЂ” 2010вЂ“11
footballers.numbers: 79 club goals В· 30 caps for Morocco
didYouKnow: Played for France at youth level before switching allegiance to Morocco in 2009.
Has publicly said signing for Tottenham instead of Arsenal was the biggest mistake of his career вЂ” blamed agent Damien Comolli's advice.
Reinvented himself at Benfica after years in the wilderness, winning the Primeira Liga in 2019 as a deep midfielder.

footballers.name: Charlie Adam
footballers.countryFlag: рџЏґу Ѓ§у Ѓўу Ѓіу ЃЈу Ѓґу Ѓї
footballers.story: The Scottish set-piece merchant who carried Blackpool through their fairytale Premier League season, parlayed it into a Liverpool move, and ended up as Stoke's resident hammer-of-a-left-foot. Capable of moments of genius вЂ” and a sixty-five yarder against Chelsea.
footballers.numbers: 80 club goals В· 26 caps for Scotland
didYouKnow: Scored a 65-yard goal against Chelsea in 2015 from inside his own half вЂ” called it a "once in a lifetime" strike.
His Stoke teammates secretly nicknamed him "Parched" вЂ” a long-running joke he didn't find out about for years until Peter Crouch revealed it on his podcast.
Captained Dundee to promotion in 2021 and called it the highlight of his career вЂ” above his Rangers Champions League nights.

footballers.name: Danny Rose
footballers.countryFlag: рџЏґу Ѓ§у Ѓўу ЃҐу Ѓ®у Ѓ§у Ѓї
footballers.story: Spurs' rocket-fuelled left-back through the Pochettino era вЂ” full-throttle, lung-busting, and capable of one of the most thunderous Premier League debut goals you'll ever see (a screamer against Arsenal). Spoke openly about depression at a time when very few footballers did.
honours: PFA Team of the Year вЂ” 2015вЂ“16, 2016вЂ“17
UEFA Nations League third place вЂ” England, 2018вЂ“19
footballers.numbers: 9 club goals in 232 appearances В· 29 caps for England
didYouKnow: His Premier League debut goal against Arsenal in 2010 вЂ” a left-footed volley from outside the box вЂ” won Sky Sports' Goal of the Season.
Publicly disclosed his battle with depression in 2018, becoming one of the most prominent active footballers to do so.
Was racially abused throughout a 2012 England U21 game in Serbia and sent off for retaliating after the final whistle вЂ” the resulting investigation became a flashpoint in European football.

footballers.name: Fabrizio Ravanelli
footballers.story: The White Feather. Juventus's snarling Champions League winner who, in a piece of mid-90s football madness, turned up at Middlesbrough and scored a hat-trick on his Premier League debut. The shirt-over-the-head celebration belonged to him before it belonged to anyone else.
footballers.numbers: 190 club goals В· 8 in 22 for Italy
didYouKnow: Nicknamed "the White Feather" because of his prematurely white hair вЂ” inherited the moniker from Juve legend Roberto Bettega.
Scored all five in Juve's 5вЂ“1 demolition of CSKA Sofia in September 1994.
His shirt-over-the-head celebration was so iconic that FIFA later changed the rules to make it a yellow-card offence.

footballers.name: Geovanni
footballers.story: The Brazilian who scored Hull City's first ever Premier League goal вЂ” and looked, for one season, like the most exotic thing the KC Stadium had ever seen. Free-kick specialist, hip-swivelling dribbler, briefly a Barcelona player. Hull's all-time most unlikely cult hero.
footballers.numbers: 52 club goals in 341 appearances
didYouKnow: Scored Hull City's first ever Premier League goal on his debut against Fulham in August 2008 вЂ” also took Man of the Match.
Was twice Benfica's Player of the Year and curled in a famous late free-kick in the Lisbon derby that sealed Champions League qualification.
Signed by San Jose Earthquakes in 2010 as their first ever Designated Player вЂ” a brief but historically notable MLS stint.

footballers.name: Morten Gamst Pedersen
footballers.story: Blackburn's Norwegian left foot вЂ” nine years at Ewood Park, hundreds of inswinging deliveries, and a knack for the spectacular. Never the quickest, never the flashiest, but you knew exactly what you were getting: dead-ball danger and a left peg straight from the pages of an old coaching manual.
footballers.numbers: 118 goals in 633 club appearances В· 17 in 83 for Norway
didYouKnow: His 2006 Euro qualifier goal against Hungary was so reminiscent of van Basten's 1988 final volley that the Norwegian media nicknamed him "van Gamsten".
Was at the centre of one of the Premier League's odder scuffles when Mikel Arteta poked him in the cheek and then the eye after a tackle in 2010.
Has Sami heritage and fronted a Norwegian footballer boyband called "The Players" вЂ” released a single for an anti-crime charity campaign.

footballers.name: Mark Schwarzer
footballers.story: Middlesbrough's Australian wall for over a decade, then Fulham's, then a quiet medal-collecting spell at Chelsea in his forties. The most appearance-clocking non-British keeper the Premier League has ever seen вЂ” and a man who somehow kept playing top-flight football until he was 43.
honours: AFC Asian Cup runner-up вЂ” Australia, 2011
footballers.numbers: 150+ Premier League clean sheets В· first non-Briton to 500 PL appearances
didYouKnow: At 41, he became the oldest player ever to debut in the Champions League when Chelsea played Steaua BucureИ™ti in December 2013 вЂ” kept a clean sheet too.
His 514 Premier League appearances make him the highest-appearing non-British player in the league's history.
His son Julian is also a goalkeeper and is eligible to represent the Philippines through his Filipino mother.

footballers.name: Benni McCarthy
footballers.story: South Africa's all-time top scorer, a Champions League winner with Mourinho's Porto, and the man who gave Blackburn Rovers a centre-forward of genuine pedigree for four seasons. Spiky, confident, deadly when he was on it.
honours: South African Premiership вЂ” Orlando Pirates, 2011вЂ“12
footballers.numbers: 32 international goals (South Africa's all-time top scorer)
didYouKnow: The only South African ever to win the Champions League вЂ” scored crucial goals against Manchester United in Porto's run.
Scored four goals in 13 minutes against Namibia at AFCON 1998, en route to sharing the tournament's Golden Boot and winning Player of the Tournament.
Won league titles in three different countries on two continents (Netherlands, Portugal, South Africa) вЂ” a first for a South African footballer.

footballers.name: Brad Friedel
footballers.story: The American who outlasted everyone. Liverpool, Blackburn, Villa, Spurs вЂ” and 310 consecutive Premier League appearances, a record that may never be broken. Once scored a goal from open play in 2004, just because he could.
honours: PFA Premier League Team of the Year вЂ” 2002вЂ“03
U.S. Soccer Athlete of the Year вЂ” 2002
U.S. National Soccer Hall of Fame вЂ” 2018
footballers.numbers: 450 Premier League appearances В· 310 consecutive PL games (record) В· 84 caps for the USMNT
didYouKnow: Scored a goal from open play for Blackburn in 2004 вЂ” one of the rare goalkeeper-from-open-play goals in Premier League history.
Saved two penalties during open play at the 2002 World Cup вЂ” only the second keeper ever to do so in the tournament. Earned the nickname "the Human Wall".
Was repeatedly denied a UK work permit by the Home Office вЂ” Forest, Newcastle and Sunderland all tried to sign him before Liverpool finally got him through in 1997.

footballers.name: Niko KranjДЌar
footballers.story: The silky Croatian playmaker who followed Harry Redknapp around for a decade вЂ” Portsmouth, Tottenham, QPR, three different clubs, three different reunions. Hailed at home as "the next ProsineДЌki". Never quite delivered in the Premier League, but moments of class were never in doubt.
honours: Croatian league Г— 2 вЂ” Dinamo (2002вЂ“03), Hajduk (2004вЂ“05)
footballers.numbers: 61 club goals В· 81 caps for Croatia
didYouKnow: His move from Dinamo Zagreb to bitter rivals Hajduk Split in 2005 was so divisive that 200 candles were lit in mourning outside his home вЂ” and 10,000 fans showed up for his Hajduk unveiling.
Joined up with Harry Redknapp at three different clubs (Portsmouth, Tottenham, QPR) вЂ” one of the great manager-player Premier League marriages.
Comes from football royalty вЂ” his father Zlatko coached Croatia to the 1998 World Cup semi-final.

footballers.name: Sylvain Distin
footballers.story: The Premier League's most-capped foreign outfielder вЂ” and yet not a single France cap to show for it. Manchester City, Portsmouth, Everton вЂ” calm, dominant in the air, the kind of defender every relegation-threatened side wished they had. The best French centre-half never to play for France.
honours: Coupe de la Ligue вЂ” Gueugnon, 2000
footballers.numbers: 402+ Premier League appearances (most by any foreign outfielder) В· 0 France caps
didYouKnow: Despite playing more Premier League games than any other foreign outfielder, he was never picked by France вЂ” announced his "international retirement" on Twitter in 2014, deadpan.
Was approached to represent Guadeloupe at the 2011 CONCACAF Gold Cup but turned it down to focus on Everton's preseason.
Assisted Tim Howard's freak 100-yard goal in 2011вЂ“12 вЂ” only the fourth keeper-from-open-play goal in Premier League history.

footballers.name: Paul Konchesky
footballers.countryFlag: рџЏґу Ѓ§у Ѓўу ЃҐу Ѓ®у Ѓ§у Ѓї
footballers.story: The East End left-back of the early 2000s вЂ” a Charlton academy product who somehow ended up at Liverpool in the absolute worst-managed transfer window of the Roy Hodgson era. Two England caps. One pie shop. No nonsense.
honours: FA Cup runner-up вЂ” West Ham, 2005вЂ“06
footballers.numbers: 14 club goals В· 2 England caps
didYouKnow: Was Charlton's youngest ever first-team player at 16 years and 93 days вЂ” a record later broken by Jonjo Shelvey.
His brief Liverpool spell is remembered for an error against Spurs that gifted Aaron Lennon the winner вЂ” and a famously unhappy public reaction from his mother on Facebook.
Opened a pie and mash cafГ© in Brentwood in 2015, with his mum on the staff вЂ” the most East End post-football career imaginable.

footballers.name: Fernando Llorente
footballers.story: El Rey LeГіn. Athletic Bilbao's towering Basque number 9 in the Marcelo Bielsa era вЂ” all elbows, headers and physical authority. Won everything with Spain's golden generation, won Serie A at Juventus, and ended his career as Tottenham's most physically improbable super-sub.
footballers.numbers: 151 club goals В· 7 in 24 for Spain
didYouKnow: Born in Pamplona but qualified for Athletic Bilbao through his Navarrese roots вЂ” stayed at the club until he was 28 before finally leaving for Juventus.
Got just 8 minutes of the 2019 Champions League final against Liverpool вЂ” his second European final cameo (also lost the 2015 final with Juventus).
Reinvented himself as a late-career super-sub at Spurs and Napoli вЂ” defenders simply couldn't deal with his aerial presence in the last 20 minutes.

footballers.name: Michu
footballers.story: One season of pure magic. 22 goals for newly-promoted Swansea, the best ВЈ2m signing in Premier League history, the player every Sunday-league team wanted to compare themselves to. Then his ankle gave out and he was gone вЂ” football's most beautiful and brief shooting star.
honours: Swansea Player of the Year вЂ” 2012вЂ“13
footballers.numbers: 22 goals in his Premier League debut season В· 83 club goals total
didYouKnow: Bought by Swansea for around ВЈ2m from Rayo Vallecano вЂ” widely considered one of the best-value Premier League signings ever.
Bought shares in his boyhood club Real Oviedo in 2012 alongside Cazorla and Mata to help them avoid financial collapse.
Forced into early retirement at 31 by chronic ankle problems вЂ” said in 2022 that he has "the ankle of a 90-year-old" despite multiple operations.

footballers.name: Ray Parlour
footballers.countryFlag: рџЏґу Ѓ§у Ѓўу ЃҐу Ѓ®у Ѓ§у Ѓї
footballers.story: The Romford PelГ©. Twelve years at Arsenal, three Premier League titles, four FA Cups вЂ” Parlour was the engine room of the Wenger years that nobody outside the squad ever quite gave proper credit to. Ran further than anyone, never complained, scored a 30-yarder in an FA Cup final.
footballers.numbers: 32 club goals В· 10 caps for England
didYouKnow: Opened the 2002 FA Cup final with a 30-yard screamer вЂ” prompting Tim Lovejoy's now-legendary commentary line: "Oh, it's all right, it's only Ray Parlour."
Scored a 30-yard strike against Finland in 2000 that clearly crossed the line вЂ” wrongly disallowed by a linesman in one of the era's most notorious refereeing errors.
His nickname "the Romford PelГ©" was self-deprecating, originally coined as a joke by Marc Overmars вЂ” and stuck for the rest of his career.

footballers.name: Ricardo Carvalho
footballers.story: The most underrated centre-back of his generation. Won the Champions League with Porto, the Premier League three times with Chelsea, La Liga with Real Madrid вЂ” and Euro 2016 with Portugal at 38. Wherever Mourinho went, Carvalho followed.
footballers.numbers: 19 club goals in 433 appearances В· 89 caps for Portugal
didYouKnow: Followed JosГ© Mourinho from Porto to Chelsea in 2004, then again from Inter to Real Madrid in 2010 вЂ” one of the great manager-player partnerships of the era.
Retired from international football in 2011 saying he'd been "disrespected", then came back nearly three years later and won Euro 2016 with Portugal at 38.
Was given a seven-month suspended prison sentence in Spain in 2017 for tax fraud during his Real Madrid years вЂ” a fate that befell several Spain-based stars of his era.

footballers.name: Wes Brown
footballers.countryFlag: рџЏґу Ѓ§у Ѓўу ЃҐу Ѓ®у Ѓ§у Ѓї
footballers.story: Manchester United's quiet, two-footed Mancunian вЂ” Sir Alex's go-to fill-in defender for over a decade. Five league titles, two Champions Leagues, never the headline act, always there when needed. The kind of squad player every great team is built on.
honours: Premier League Г— 5 вЂ” Manchester United
Champions League Г— 2 вЂ” Manchester United, 2007вЂ“08 & (squad) 1998вЂ“99
FA Cup Г— 2 В· League Cup Г— 2
13 trophies in total at Old Trafford
footballers.numbers: 6 career goals В· 23 caps for England
didYouKnow: Sir Alex Ferguson once said Brown was "without question the best natural defender this club has had for years" вЂ” high praise for a player whose career was largely about quiet reliability.
Won the Jimmy Murphy Young Player of the Year award twice as a Manchester United youth вЂ” only Ryan Giggs had previously done it.

Was sent off in 2015 for a foul his Sunderland teammate John O'Shea had actually committed вЂ” the red card was rescinded on appeal but the moment became one of the strangest dismissals in Premier League history.
